title: Level 145
wikitext:

"Schicksalszeichen"
"Schicksalszeichen"
You reflect on the past hour. You realize it was all because of that one paper slip. You knew that it was your wrongdoing, as that paper would have done nothing had you not signed. You're a decently intelligent person. Being astute is invaluable in a place like the Backrooms where inconsistency is king. Your mind was quite apt at taking on and adapting to its trials and tribulations. Armed with it, you'd smell a betrayal or a scam from a mile away; they are quite commonplace in this environment after all, whether it would be from the Backrooms itself or from its many uncivilized inhabitants forced to call it home. And yet, this is where it ends. A mere paper slip was all it took. You looked at its half-baked vague promise. You don't know where it'd take you to, just that it'd be better than your current life of constant guard duty. With wages that hardly suffice for a sip, you always pondered how it got this low. You kept thinking yourself that this would be an acceptable, even preferable fate for many. But deep down, you knew that you needed a step up. At the very least, you'd want something to happen, when your whole purpose is to see nothing happen most of the time. That's where the slip got you. The settlement was short-staffed after an entity attack. In their absence, your shifts became longer, and you needed to secure more hallways. You felt the weight of the world on the shells of your shotgun. The air was heavy with the cacophony of your breaths while the fluorescent radiance crawled through your crimsoned eyes. Seeing the barracks again at the close of a shift was the highlight of the day. Just four shifts was all it takes for you to snap, but was halted by the paper slip in the nick of time. Your mind is drained after the fourth shift, you gave no thought to the credibility of the slip, signing it in unfiltered hope. Looking forward to a general improvement in your life, you tuck yourself beneath the ragged thin cloth over a tattered mattress. A blinding spotlight had assaulted those well-rested eyes of yours. It profoundly pierced your perception, drawing your full attention to it even once you covered your eyes with your arms. It distracted you from the realization that you were laying on smooth, solid ground. It was increasingly clear that where you woke up does not match where you slept. You crawled sideways to your right to escape the spotlight, beyond which was a striking nothingness. The spotlight followed you no matter where you went, yet your eyes adapted to it; thank God you didn't look straight up. Eventually, after a few minutes, you had fully awakened. Standing on a pristine white floor you can't glean the material of, illuminated by a homing spotlight. The smell of a new car perforated the air, which had taken you back to favored memories of that one road trip back home. You basked in the mild breeze of the air, a stark contrast from the freezing, tight hallways that were the hallmark of overworked mundaneness. As your vision fixated onto the gaping void that surrounds, another spotlight interrupted it, along with a heavy click that delivered a consuming echo. At the cynosure of the spotlight stood a white table and a monobloc chair. On the table are a stack of papers and a black pen. You figured that of all of the things that could manifest in the void, this was an odd selection. Something that would grab your attention no matter where else you'd be, be it [[Level 4]] or just a regular office back home. It is as if the stack of a hundred or so papers were just asking to be investigated. And so you do, slowly approaching the table, cautious yet curious. You took a seat and read what is on the top page.



WELCOME TO
TEST-145 145 pages, six questions each.
RULES
'''1.''' Read the questions carefully and astutely.
'''2.''' The test has no time limit
'''3.''' You must write your answer on the box provided below the question.
'''4.''' You are only allowed to write with pens from the box of pens to your right.
'''5.''' Once you are finished with the paper, place it to your left.
'''6.''' Attempting to erase an answer automatically marks it wrong.
'''7.''' You are only allowed nine mistakes. Mistakes will be marked by a red overhead floodlight in front of you.
'''8.''' Do not tamper with the test apparatus.
'''9.''' Do not skip a page with an unanswered question.
'''10.''' Weeping and gnashing of teeth are permitted.
'''11.''' Only God shall provide assistance.
But you weren't weeping. Or gnashing your teeth. As much as you could, you calmed yourself down, and deeply thought about where it all went wrong. You say to yourself about how this was almost bound to happen. The slip got you where you were at your weakest. You cannot blame yourself for the predicament you are currently in. Once all of that is over, you rush to conclusions on what the exam could be like. It could be anything ranging from kindergarten all the way to Harvard, or even beyond. There may be questions the rest of mankind shall yet ask. You stopped, and proceeded to ask what would exactly be the consequences of acquiring ten violations on the test, but it's not worth worrying. There is only one reliable way to approach these questions, and that is to answer the test. There's only one way out of this mess, no lucky noclipping or other act of God. You put the first page away and start answering. {{Collapse|num=1|initial=
🖊
🖊
|final=   |body= The moment you process the contents of the opening pages, you've already solved them. Your brain turns to autopilot as it faces the challenges of calculating two plus five, remembering the number of fingers on a singular human hand, and reciting the alphabet. Challenges easily conquered by almost anyone with a functioning brain. You blaze through the pages as if they were orderly waiting to be processed by you in mere seconds. Answering these questions makes you feel like a machine. Even some of the questions repeat themselves, with minimal surface-level variations from their previous iterations. You finish five pages, then ten, then twenty. After a couple dozen pages finished, you can't help but notice. This is too good to be true. Chances are the very next page is all about algebra, Auriellen history, or even questions no human or perhaps being will ever answer. Yet you still breeze through every page. You can't help but notice, that there has been a gradual difficulty slope. The questions become more of speed bumps, keeping you alert yet you still stomp them with ease. Maybe there won't be any difficulty spike, and it will be one slow rise to the top, a top which you are still yet to glean. The steady rise in difficulty hasn't quite risen your guard as you rush through the pages. Hurriedly, you finish one of the questions, dragging the pen too hard on the paper, leaving behind a tiny rift. Suddenly, a red overhead light in front lights up, along with the same click from before. It looms over, serving as a stark reminder to avoid haste. You have learned the hard way, but it is better than not learning at all. You write noticeably slower, with more thought and patience. For only God knows what awaits you when you incur your tenth infraction. Thirty pages have passed. You've gotten every question right so far, just not your grip over the pen. The questions you're facing now aren't exactly difficult, but they make you stop and think. You can't help but think that you could attribute that sensation to the rift earlier. When faced with questions like "what's the prevalent anomaly hindering documentation of Level 12?" or "how many stars are there on the American flag?" you think to yourself that even if you would still get these questions right, many would have faltered by now and faced the consequences. But that's only under the assumption that everyone gets the same paper that you do. Someone's first paper would be about calculus or niche dates in history. But that's also under the naive assumption that you're not the first wanderer facing this challenge. You may be the first to take the test, but hopefully also the first to tell the tale. In an area whose whole purpose is to question you, you can't help but also question it. You ponder "why am I in the middle of a pitch-black void? What is the purpose of this test? What are the consequences for not passing? And to answer those questions, you must first determine the product of 448 divided by 16. Long division wasn't your strong suit, but you had other ways of improvising around which were cumbersome. You're thankful that time isn't of the essence for this test. That assumption is reinforced by the fact that you're not feeling hungry or thirsty. You weren't fed very well at your job back "home", and with you sitting around doing all this busywork, you'd see a half-eaten sandwich as a Thanksgiving dinner. Soon enough, the road turns from smoothly paved with a few speedbumps to coarse gravel. It isn't exactly a trail in the forested wilderness quite yet, but you know that it's easier now to screw up. Some questions leave you stumped for a moment, and some leave you resorting to guesswork. But guesswork can only get you so far. So you incur your second infraction, on the forty-fourth page, on a question about what is the study of classifying living things called. You knew that it sounded similar to something about the government taking a cut off your sorry ass, but you butchered the spelling and bam. Well at least it was your first infraction that stemmed from a genuine gap of knowledge. There's no blaming on this one. All you have to do is just move on and pray. Page 58. An entire page dedicated to memorizing pivotal historic dates in the Frontrooms. When did D-Day take place? When was Abraham Lincoln shot? On what day did the Great War end? History was something that intrigued you when you were still back in the Frontrooms, but you hated when the curriculum consisted of memorizing specific dates. There may be a lot to learn from history, but dates are only a superficial aspect and focusing on it is mere tedium. It was only worse for the others who weren't interested in history at all, who think it is useless to be up to date on the past. After thinking about this for a while and rummaging through your brain as much as you can, you instinctively put the page away, onto the next. Immediately, a loud click floods the air. You then notice a faint shade of red on the table and paper. You look up, and see that you've gone from two mistakes accrued to two mistakes remaining. The simple act of sliding an unwritten test sheet to your left quadrupled the overhead floodlights. Any heart rate monitors - if you somehow had them on your body that is, would also be on red alert. You fail to take it all in. You're over there, panting, shedding a few tears. You're mind is moving so fast, yet it goes nowhere. In its haze though, one thing is very clear to you. You may be staring at death right at its very face. So you contemplate, what does it mean to fail TEST-145? The consequences of the fate present themselves to be pretty grim. There were mentions that you could weep and gnash your teeth, and pray like your soul's trudging along the narrow path. Death, be it swift and sheer or terrorizing and torturous, might be a merciful option and the only real escape in your grasp. What follows after your passing may lead to either the highest of highs to the lowest of lows, all in between. Succeeding in the test, something that you can no longer consider a real possibility, may be the only true guarantee that this place will let you free. But maybe, you could at least try. You still have a chance, and so you look to Page 59. The following pages posed questions that thankfully tested critical thinking, application of gained knowledge, and even multiple choice, and not memorization. It was the bane of every student both here and back home in the Frontrooms. You knew that if school actually did teach, if school actually did provide valuable lessons that would be useful to our lives, then that would mean the general populace would be too smart and powerful for those in charge to control. By shoving random knowledge about science, math, history, and English, which most would not need to apply, provided they still retain such skills in their career, they keep everyone powerless while still ensuring that they are "learners" and "achievers". After a dozen or so pages though, you make another mistake. The pages still ramp up in difficulty, and no matter how much of them you get right, by this point you are bound to get at least one of the questions wrong. Now, one mistake is all that is left. You've made the most amount of mistakes before you face the consequences. And you're only halfway through the entire test, on what is presumedly its easiest half. It's best to say that there is no escape from this madness. The chances of you getting through the other half without any mistakes is astronomically slim. You sense that it is best to see how far you can make it before making another mistake, so you do not fold, and you keep pushing on. Page 75. You look through every question with extreme care. You are never quick to answer. Shockingly, your memories are at the best that it's ever been, as if the level is holding your hand, like a catwalk over a bottomless pit with one balustrade. The questions are certainly challenging, and you are never sure if you'll get the answer right. Thankfully there haven't been any math questions in a while, which just stopped earlier at basic algebra. Surprisingly they're about topics you've enjoyed or hardly struggled with. They're still challenging for the average person, so you know that if your interests diverged in the past, you would have kicked the bucket a long time ago. You can't help but feel that this is too good to be true, and that a switch-up may follow upon the next page turn.
O,B, _ , _ ,G,K,M
Fill in the blanks. But you're the one that's blank. You don't even know what it may represent. There's nothing about this sequence of letters that you can glean. You have absolutely no clue what to do anymore, except face reality for what it is. With one final guess, one final shot in the dark, you try to fill in the letters with what seems best to you. There's only a 1 in 676 chance that you get it right, assuming that the answer is indeed letters in the Latin alphabet. After making the shot, you incur your final infraction. All 10 red foglights light up the space. ... {{Audio|file=Penultimaschön.mp3|volume=0.725|secrettext=the one true shabad|align=center|mono=yes}} Suddenly, after the music starts playing, a cascade of papers from all directions commences. The last sounds you'll hear are the cacophony of a billion ruffling papers and the electronic beats of the song exemplifying the ultimate failure. There's no point in using the papers to rise to the top, you'll reach the roof anyway. With no noclipping to save you, there is only one fate ahead. The flood is delayed. The papers pile up in all directions, with some eventually reaching heights taller than you, but you're still there with the papers barely making it past your ankle. Not too many of the papers landed near you yet. There's no more time to go through the five stages of grief anymore. As the papers slowly rise up, brushing against your clothes and skin, you just stand there, watching your fate unfold. After a couple hours, when you can't survive the weight of a growing landfill of papers on your body, like having no suit at the bottom of the ocean floor, one final thought comes to exemplify your journey and your thoughts along the way. This is where absolute certainty leads. }}
